source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.3-9.4 types and permissions (pp. 160-161)

options: It is started by the system, associated with no terminal, owned by a system user (often root), and waits in memory for a call
It is always started from a user's terminal
It only runs when a user logs in
questions.why: A daemon is launched by the system, tied to no terminal, owned by a system user (often root), loaded at boot and waiting in memory.

questions.q: How is a daemon usually recognised by its name, and what does 'daemon' stand for?
options: By a trailing 'd' (e.g. sshd, crond); Disk And Execution MONitor
By an uppercase name; Dedicated Autonomous Entity MONitor
By a '.service' suffix; Data Access MONitor
questions.why: Daemons typically end in 'd' (sshd, crond); the term stands for Disk And Execution MONitor.

questions.q: By default, what are a process's real UID and GID?
options: The same as the real UID/GID of the user who executed the command
questions.why: Normally the process's real UID/GID equal those of the user who ran the command.

questions.q: What changes when a command has the SUID bit set?
options: The process's EFFECTIVE UID becomes the command owner's, not the user's - so effective and real UIDs differ
The command can no longer be run
questions.why: SUID (or SGID) makes the effective UID (or GID) that of the command's owner; the system checks access by the EFFECTIVE identifiers.
